title: practical english usage - Part 148

Things can be more or less difficult or important; people can (e.g. difficult, important, happy, tired) are words for qualities that exist in Adjectives can be divided into gradable and non-gradable. I'm the tallest in my family.  We bought the cheapest.

Nouns are often left out after superlative adjectives.

For the structures that are possible with a particular adjective, see a good dictionary.

un derstand person.) not    e s He's a difficult person to understand. (     H  ' a difficult to We   rarely put adjective + complement before a noun (►   186.5).

I'm pleased that we seem to agree.

I'm pleased about her promotion. I'm pleased to see you here.

And many adjectives can have more than one kind of complement.

It's important that everybody should feel comfortable.

I'm glad that you were able to come.

Some adjectives can be followed by clauses (►  264).

I'm anxious for her to get a good education. (= I’ m  anxious that she An infinitive may have its own subject, introduced by for (►   113).

You don't look happy to see me. The soup is ready to eat.

Some can be followed by infinitives (►    101).

I'm interested in cookery. I'm interested in learning to cook.

complement. Some can be followed by preposition + noun/-ing  (►   103).
